snippet:
- name: Run tests
        env:
          TREASURY_REST_TEST_JDBC_URL: jdbc:mariadb://127.0.0.1:3306/treasury_rest_test
          TREASURY_REST_TEST_DB_USER: root
          TREASURY_REST_TEST_DB_PASS: treasury
        run: ./gradlew --no-daemon :treasury-rest-api:test -Pci=true
description: treasury-rest-api/build.gradle.kts (lines 96-111) wires a JaCoCo coverage ratchet (LINE COVEREDRATIO >= 0.55) into `check` via jacocoTestCoverageVerification, and the header comment says the money-handling service must not ship untested. But the only CI job that runs it (treasury-rest-api-test.yml, reused by treasury-rest-api-docker.yml which builds the bootJar with `-x test`) invokes only `:treasury-rest-api:test` — never `jacocoTestReport` or `jacocoTestCoverageVerification`, and never `check`. The declared coverage gate on the money service is therefore dead in CI: coverage can regress arbitrarily and no pipeline fails. This is the treasury-rest-api analogue of the documented chestshop gap, but here the gate protects the money endpoint transfer engine.
recommendation: Add `:treasury-rest-api:jacocoTestReport :treasury-rest-api:jacocoTestCoverageVerification` (or `:treasury-rest-api:check`) to the Run tests step in treasury-rest-api-test.yml so the 0.55 ratchet actually gates PRs and the Docker build.
verification_note: Snippet matches verbatim at treasury-rest-api-test.yml:61-66. Claim holds: build.gradle.kts:96-111 wires a 0.55 LINE COVEREDRATIO jacocoTestCoverageVerification into check, but the only CI job (treasury-rest-api-test.yml) runs `:treasury-rest-api:test -Pci=true` with no jacocoTestReport/jacocoTestCoverageVerification/check; the docker workflow (treasury-rest-api-docker.yml:26) reuses that same test workflow and builds bootJar with `-x test` (line 56), so the gate is never enforced in any pipeline. By contrast treasury-test.yml:72 and business-test.yml:73 explicitly append the two jacoco tasks, confirming the omission is real and asymmetric. However CLAUDE.md explicitly documents this exact gap ('chestshop/rest-api CI runs test, not the verification task — run them locally'), so it is a known CI-hygiene limitation with no runtime/correctness impact and a locally-runnable gate — below major. Downgraded to minor.

category: Convention plugin bypassed / duplicated JVM config
description: The build-logic convention plugin io.paradaux.jvm-conventions owns exactly the Java toolchain (JavaLanguageVersion.of(21)) plus JavaCompile encoding=UTF-8 and release=21. Three JVM subprojects that do NOT apply it re-declare that same block inline instead: treasury/treasury-api (toolchain + encoding + release), business/business-api (toolchain, lines 11-17), and treasury-rest-api (toolchain, lines 12-16). This is the copy-pasted-subprojects pattern the charter flags: the JDK-21 baseline lives in four places (the convention plugin + three inline copies), so a version bump must touch all of them and they can silently diverge. common applies the convention correctly and is the model to follow.
recommendation: Apply id("io.paradaux.jvm-conventions") in treasury-api, business-api, and treasury-rest-api and delete the inline java.toolchain / JavaCompile.release blocks, leaving only the module-specific extras (withSourcesJar/withJavadocJar, reproducible-jar flags).

description: The Shadow plugin version 9.0.2 is hardcoded in two build files that must stay in lockstep: the root build.gradle.kts `id("com.gradleup.shadow") version "9.0.2" apply false` (line 14) and build-logic/build.gradle.kts `implementation("com.gradleup.shadow:shadow-gradle-plugin:9.0.2")` (line 23). The build-logic dependency exists precisely so the paper-server convention can reference the ShadowJar task type, and the comment even notes the two versions must match — but nothing enforces it; a bump in one place silently drifts from the other. More broadly the charter states gradle/libs.versions.toml should own every plugin version, yet all Gradle-plugin versions (shadow 9.0.2, spring-boot 4.1.0, dependency-management 1.1.7 in the root build; flyway 12.9.0 in economy-flyway) live inline rather than in the catalog, so there is no single source of truth for plugin versions.
recommendation: Add a [plugins]/[versions] entry for shadow in libs.versions.toml and reference it from both the root build and build-logic (e.g. via libs.versions.shadow.get() for the build-logic classpath dep), so 9.0.2 is defined once; migrate the other plugin versions into the catalog for a single source of truth.

occurrences.snippet:
plugins {
    id("com.gradleup.shadow") version "9.0.2" apply false
    id("org.springframework.boot") version "4.1.0" apply false
    id("io.spring.dependency-management") version "1.1.7" apply false

description: The publish-target repository block (snapshot/release URL selection + REPO_USER/REPO_PASS env credentials) that supplies the maven-publish repository for the api submodules is duplicated byte-for-byte between treasury/build.gradle.kts (lines 192-215) and business/build.gradle.kts (lines 177-200) — verified identical by diff. This is shared publishing logic that a convention plugin should own; the two copies can drift (e.g. a repo-host change applied to one and not the other), and each api module (treasury-api, business-api) declares its own publications yet relies on the parent's copy-pasted repository wiring.
recommendation: Extract the publish-repository block into a shared convention plugin (e.g. io.paradaux.published-library-conventions) applied by treasury-api and business-api, so the paradaux.io snapshot/release repo and env-var credentials are declared once.

snippet:
- name: Set up JDK 21
        uses: actions/setup-java@v4
        with:
          distribution: temurin
          java-version: '21'

      - name: Setup Gradle
        uses: gradle/actions/setup-gradle@v4

      - name: Make Gradle wrapper executable
        run: chmod +x ./gradlew
description: gradle/actions/wrapper-validation (which verifies the committed gradle-wrapper.jar against known-good checksums before it is executed) is applied inconsistently: the publish and docker workflows and chestshop/api-plugin build validate the wrapper, but treasury-test.yml, business-test.yml, chestshop-test.yml, treasury-rest-api-test.yml, treasury-build.yml and business-build.yml all run ./gradlew without validating the wrapper jar first. Every one of these still executes the same checked-in wrapper jar, so a tampered wrapper would run unchecked in the majority of the JVM CI jobs. The protection is only as strong as its least-guarded runner.
recommendation: Add a `gradle/actions/wrapper-validation@v4` step (or a single reusable setup composite) to the six Gradle workflows that currently omit it, so every workflow that runs ./gradlew validates the wrapper jar first.
